## Meeting Overview

Vrijen Attawar and Anna Bao met for an initial planning session focused on FOHI's 2026 New York City chapter strategy. The meeting opened with personal context — Vrijen is visiting friends in Raleigh while Anna house-hunts in NYC from Astoria to Greenpoint. Anna walked Vrijen through recent member survey results (55 responses from ~800 members) and outlined a comprehensive event calendar for the year, a three-tier volunteer structure, and the sponsorship push — culminating in Vrijen taking full ownership of the February happy hour as his first leadership deliverable.

## Chronological Discussion

### Personal Context and Geographic Logistics (0:00–3:30)

The conversation opened with Vrijen apologizing for a brief delay — he's at a friend's house in Raleigh on a two-day escape and dealing with new WiFi. He'd changed his flight to avoid the incoming snowstorm, canceling plans to do a Zo session with Zoe and prep for a Next Play workshop presentation (already at 80 signups). Anna shared she's staying in NYC through the weekend to house-hunt, renting in the corridor from Astoria down to Greenpoint. Vrijen noted the transportation gap between that area and his downtown Brooklyn location — he's been cabbing $40 round-trip to a Hearth pod in Williamsburg for Ray's community, and joked that "Zoron's next winning platform" should be connecting the Williamsburg elite with the downtown Brooklyn intelligentsia. This geography thread foreshadowed the neighborhood ambassador concept later in the conversation.

### FOHI Onboarding and Survey Walkthrough (4:00–9:00)

Vrijen mentioned he'd had a good onboarding call with Alex and Ray about FOHI the previous day. Anna then shared her screen to walk through the chapter survey (55 of ~800 members responded). Key demographic findings:

- **Dominant segments**: Edtech and freelancers/consultants — "a surprising amount of consultants"
- **Represented sectors**: Administration, faculty, philanthropy, investing, journalism, policy
- **Growth opportunity**: HR and L&D remain underrepresented — Anna flagged this as a pipeline to cultivate
- **Event preferences**: Panels, happy hours, co-working, conferences, Storm King-style outings, women-specific events, hackathons, AI-focused workshops, debates, and notably a running meetup
- **Communication**: Strong email preference alongside Slack — Anna proposed a quarterly newsletter with event highlights, side-event callouts, member spotlights, and chat snippets
- **Topics of interest**: AI adoption, evolving skill workshops, education financing, data and impact, policy/regulations, new models of higher education, fundraising — with edtech company topics surprisingly low-priority
- **Volunteer interest**: Strong pool — neighborhood ambassadors, connectors, greeters, and facilitators all well-represented

Anna had already started outreach to Modern Day (a nonprofit surfaced in partnership suggestions) and a few other organizations. She emphasized this was only 55 responses but likely representative of the broader base.

### 2026 Event Calendar and Strategic Initiatives (10:00–16:00)

Anna outlined the year's event roadmap:

- **February**: Happy hour — simple, people just want to meet. Vrijen to own this entirely.
- **March**: Hackathon — planning should begin now if targeting March.
- **April**: Collaborative debate event with **Unmuted** (FYI) — a "discussion and social club for the politically curious" that runs events like "Law & Power." Anna has been following the founder and likes their spicier takes. They've run higher-ed events before and are strong at pulling in local partners, which aligns with the NYC-centric focus.
- **July**: Potential **NYC Summit** (1–2 days) — the marquee initiative. Starbridge has signaled they may offer space and are also running their own event around that time. This mirrors the Boston summit done a few years ago with Dan Fohi. Ray asked Anna to raise this with Vrijen; if both agree it's worth pursuing, conversations start next week. Anna noted this is "a lot to plan for" — months of lead time required.
- **May/June**: Lighter — potentially just happy hours or volunteer-led field trips, giving room for summit prep.
- **December**: Holiday get-together.

Anna stressed that sponsorship is a major 2026 goal, and the workload will be heavily front-loaded: "a lot of reach out, a lot of talking, a lot of connecting" in the next few months. She emphasized leveraging volunteers so it's not all on two people.

### Volunteer Program Design (16:00–25:00)

Anna presented a three-tier volunteer structure — entirely new, "no one has done this before":

**1. Event Creators** — Event-specific facilitators modeled on Andrew Young's fellowship approach. Anna envisions a listserv where volunteers indicate availability per event, with a minimum commitment of just one event. Vrijen contributed that Andrew Young's fellows do this well — their whole job is to facilitate discussion, introduce people, and keep conversation flowing. He offered to speak with Jacob from the program for insider tips. Anna said she'd also taken inspiration from the Andrew Young model but hadn't looked into specifics.

**2. Connectors** — Two formats:
- **In-person**: "Social lubricants" at events (hybrid with event greeter role)
- **Virtual office hours**: Subject-expertise connectors offering one-hour slots. Members sign up based on the connector's network/expertise. Very low lift — one hour in eight months, post to the channel, people sign up via calendar link.

Vrijen referenced **Harvard iLab's office hours** as a successful precedent — established industry folks posted calendar availability and members could sign up for slots. The "feel good halo" of being associated as a FOHI expertise person gives value back to connectors.

Anna flagged constraints from Ray and Jake: they're "very hesitant" on new platforms — the lower-tech, the better. Google Sheets and calendar links, not new software. She also noted that **Donut** (Slack coffee-matching bot) already exists in FOHI, so the connector role needs to be differentiated as expertise-driven rather than random pairing.

**3. Neighborhood Ambassadors** — Facilitate localized coffee meetups and co-working in their own neighborhoods, since centralized Manhattan events don't serve everyone. This distributes the "last mile" planning to locals.

Vrijen synthesized the logic: "You want to do things where we can hand off the localized last-mile planning to someone else... so I see the logic in what you're proposing."

### Responsibility Assignment (25:00–26:14)

Anna shifted to responsibilities. Key points:
- The shared planning document is between Anna, Vrijen, and Ray only
- Ray's stance: NYC is Anna and Vrijen's jurisdiction. He'll support when asked but won't direct — "he will ask us if he can do something"
- Anna offered Vrijen full ownership of the **February happy hour** as a first challenge
- She expressed trust in Vrijen's capability: "I trust you got a good handful of yourself... surprise me"
- The transcript cuts off as Anna begins walking through a responsibility breakdown document

## Key Takeaways

- **Vrijen owns the February happy hour** — his first solo leadership deliverable for FOHI NYC
- **NYC Summit (July)** is the flagship 2026 initiative — Starbridge may host, conversations to start next week if both leads agree
- **Unmuted debate partnership (April)** in active discussions — spicier, policy-oriented programming
- **Three-tier volunteer program**: Event Creators, Connectors (expertise office hours), Neighborhood Ambassadors — all designed around low-tech, low-commitment, distributed ownership
- **Quarterly newsletter** to be created based on strong member email preference
- **Low-tech mandate** from Ray and Jake — Google Sheets and calendar links, no new platforms
- **Sponsorship is a major 2026 priority** with heavy Q1/Q2 outreach workload
- **Vrijen to gather intel** from Andrew Young's fellowship model via Jacob
- **Survey gap**: HR/L&D underrepresented — growth opportunity for targeted outreach
- **Hackathon in March** needs immediate planning attention if timeline holds
